name: Student_100 Cognitive-Level Programming Simulation Artifact
population_or_context: Python programming tasks and past learning records for students with diverse cognitive levels.
sample_size: Student_100 dataset with 100 students and 5,000 learning records.
methods: The framework builds student cognitive prototypes from past learning records, maps prototypes to new tasks, predicts likely behavior, and uses beam-search self-refinement to generate imperfect student-like programming solutions.
education_context: Programming education with constructed-response code solutions.
response_origin: LLM-agent-generated code solutions conditioned on cognitive prototypes and past learning records.
rubric_label_space: Programming solution correctness, cognitive-level alignment, and realism metrics rather than a production automated scoring rubric.
label_source: Past learning records and task outcomes define cognitive profiles and expected mistakes; human evaluation is used as an additional check.
generation_model: LLM-based agent framework; paper compares against baseline LLM prompting and reports experiments in controlled programming settings.
generation_method: Training-free cognitive prototype construction, behavior prediction, and beam-search self-refinement designed to reproduce plausible mistakes.
prompt_strategy: Prototype- and behavior-conditioned generation with iterative self-refinement rather than simple helpful-assistant prompting.
evaluation_method: Simulation accuracy, realism assessment, component ablation, analysis by past-learning-record volume, and human evaluation.
scorer_use: No automated scorer validation; the paper evaluates simulation quality for research and methodology.
real_response_comparator: Past learning records from the Student_100 dataset serve as the basis for cognitive prototypes and likely mistake patterns.
reported_metrics: The paper reports 100% improvement in simulation accuracy and realism over baselines and shows component ablations where removing cognitive prototypes, behavior prediction, or self-refinement decreases performance.
limitations: Validation is constrained to programming domains.
The simulation focuses on textual and behavioral patterns without multimodal signals.
The authors state the work is early-stage and conducted in controlled experimental settings, not deployed teaching or assessment.
